name: House Panim

street_level_faction_presence.street_signals: Symbols, uniform cues, work marks, and rumor shorthand make power visible in daily movement.

greetings: Mournful courtesy and measured acknowledgment of family lines, vows, or dead kin are common.
hospitality_rules: Guests are offered water, incense, and a seat appropriate to the gravity of their business.
insult_triggers: failing ritual respect around offerings or coffins
taboo_topics: public bargaining over memorial worth in crude terms
class_expectations: clerks and ritual brokers remain controlled and patient even under distress
faith_etiquette: offerings are handled carefully, names of the dead are spoken with deliberation, and ritual order matters
suspicious_behavior: desperate attempts to bypass ritual cost
outsider_mistakes: seeing Panim as only morbid rather than civic, devotional, and economic

occupation_and_daily_life.daily_rhythm: Daily life turns on records, mediation, offering preparation, grief traffic, and the steady movement of family duty through sacred administration.
occupation_and_daily_life.work_rhythm_by_calendar_or_season: Calendar observance and death traffic both matter; ritual surges reshape labor more than market fashion does.
occupation_and_daily_life.ordinary_texture: The air carries salt, incense, wax, cedar, linen, and low voices. Public spaces feel watched by memory, accountancy, and doctrine.

travel_and_transition_texture: Approaches to Panim feel quieter; cart traffic often includes memorial goods, and strangers quickly learn that tone matters as much as purpose.
dwelling_and_settlement_life: Homes keep ancestor spaces, mourning cloth, and clear divisions between guest reception and family grief rooms.
relationship_and_reputation_logic: People are remembered for reverence, discretion, follow-through on rites, and how they treat the bereaved.
region_specific_emotional_tone: Panim stories incline toward grief, secrecy, mediation, debt to the dead, piety under stress, and the moral cost of sacred administration.
